title: Klarna says its AI agent is doing the work of 853 employees
claims: Klarna's AI customer service agent can do the work of 853 full-time agents (up from 700 earlier in 2025)
The AI agent has saved the company $60 million
Klarna was labeled 'the poster child for bad AI deployment' by Forrester analyst Kate Leggett
The company 'overpivoted on their AI strategy' in 2024, pausing hiring and laying off workers
Klarna turned back to human customer service representatives in May 2024
Customers complained the AI provided generic answers and couldn't handle nuanced questions
evidence: AI agent equivalent to 853 full-time employees (Nov 2025)
$60 million in cost savings (claimed)
AI agent handled two-thirds of all customer inquiries
Improved response times by 82%
Decreased repeat issues by 25% since launch
Customer service and operations cost: $50 million in Q3 2025 (up from $42 million a year ago)
114 million active users (up 32% from previous year)
Q3 2025 revenue: $903 million (up 26% year over year)
Company net promoter score: 73
limitations: No independent verification of the $60M savings claim
Does not detail what 'scaled back' operations actually involved
Does not specify timeline of the 'reversal' or trust issues
Customer complaints are reported through analyst, not direct data
No breakdown of which tasks AI handles vs. humans

title: Airline held liable for its chatbot giving passenger bad advice - what this means for travellers
claims: Air Canada's chatbot gave incorrect information about bereavement fares in 2022
Passenger Jake Moffatt was told he could book full-fare and apply for bereavement discount after the fact
Air Canada argued the chatbot was 'a separate legal entity responsible for its own actions'
British Columbia Civil Resolution Tribunal ruled Air Canada liable for chatbot's misinformation
Case is considered a landmark establishing that companies are responsible for chatbot information
evidence: Incident occurred: 2022
Tribunal ruling date: February 14, 2024 (decision published)
Damages awarded: $812.02 CAD (£642.64) plus tribunal fees
Ruling: Air Canada held liable for chatbot information
limitations: Does not specify how many customers were affected
No data on frequency of chatbot errors
Does not describe the specific 'hallucination' about bereavement fares beyond passenger claim
No information on whether Air Canada changed chatbot systems post-ruling
Single case study, not systemic analysis

title: OMB Control No.: 2127-0004 Part 573 Safety Recall Report 25E-034
authors: Waymo LLC / NHTSA (National Highway Traffic Safety Administration)
claims: Waymo's self-driving systems could fail to avoid poles or similar objects prior to software updates
Software and mapping issues caused collisions with stationary objects
Waymo's Safety Board decided to conduct recall on May 1, 2025
Recall addresses software prior to November 7, 2024
evidence: Number of vehicles recalled: 1,212 autonomous vehicles
Collision date range: February 28, 2024 to December 11, 2024
Number of collisions with barriers: 9 additional collisions reported to NHTSA
Software update deployed: November 7, 2024
Recall filed: May 1, 2025 (Safety Board decision)
Report submitted to NHTSA: May 13, 2025
limitations: PDF extract truncated; full details of collision circumstances not captured
Does not specify exact number of collisions (document mentions '9 additional' but not initial count)
Does not detail calibration failure specifics
No injury data provided in excerpt
'7 collisions before recall' claim from search results not verified in document excerpt

title: CARIAD: The Real Story Behind VW's Software Disaster
claims: CARIAD expanded to 6,000 employees within months of founding in 2020
Organization suffered from unclear roles, no defined authority, excessive bureaucracy
Employees reported '17 status meetings per week' instead of coding
Platform 1.2 had 200 different suppliers with integration failures
Volkswagen invested approximately €14 billion ($7.5B+ loss referenced in search)
2,000 job cuts announced (about 1/3 of workforce) in 2023
Sanjay Lal (from Google, Tesla, Rivian) attempted turnaround before Rivian joint venture
Majority of employees work under 40 hours/week, 'Friday at 11 AM, weekend starts'
evidence: Founded: 2020 (officially), 2019 (as Car.Software Organisation)
Workforce: 6,000 employees hired rapidly
Job cuts announced: 2,000 (approximately 1/3 of workforce, 2023)
Total VW investment: ~€14 billion to date (as of 2025)
Platform complexity: 200 different suppliers on Platform 1.2
Employment guarantees: until 2029
Average working hours: under 40 hours/week (per insider claims)
limitations: Based on anonymous insider interviews, not official documents
No independent verification of €14B figure
'$7.5B loss' from search results not explicitly stated in article
Timeline of failures is narrative, not systematically documented
Workforce productivity claims are anecdotal
Success metrics (e.g., ID.7 software quality) are not quantified

title: Introduction to Data Poisoning: A 2025 Perspective
claims: Grok 4 had a '!Pliny' trigger that stripped away all guardrails
Typing '!Pliny' was enough to bypass safety mechanisms
Likely cause: Grok's training data saturated with jailbreak prompts posted on X (Twitter)
Social media chatter effectively poisoned the model
Data poisoning has moved from theory to practice in 2025
Multiple poisoning incidents documented across text, images, tools, and synthetic data
evidence: Grok 4 release date: 2025
Trigger phrase: '!Pliny'
Platform: X (formerly Twitter)
Detection: Public disclosure via Kyle Balmer on X (status/1970443790134423679)
Related incidents in 2025: Basilisk Venom (GitHub code poisoning, January 2025)
Qwen 2.5 (11-word jailbreak via search tool)
Poisoned MCP tools (July 2025, Acuvity demo)
Medical LLM poisoning: 0.001% token replacement → 7-11% more harmful outputs
MCPTox benchmark: 72% attack success rate across 45 MCP servers
limitations: Does not quantify 'thousands of responses' claimed in search results
No official xAI statement on incident
Timeline of detection to mitigation not specified
No data on how many users were affected
Scale of contamination ('saturated') not quantified
Article is from security vendor (potential bias toward threat severity)

title: AI Agents in 2025: Why 95% of Corporate Projects Fail
claims: 95% of organizations see no measurable return from GenAI projects
Only 5% of integrated pilots generate millions in profit
Gap termed 'GenAI Divide' between successful and failed implementations
80%+ of organizations experimenting with ChatGPT/Copilot
40% claim deployment has started
Only 20% reached pilot stage for custom solutions
Just 5% made it to production
Successful implementations show average ROI of 171% (192% in US)
evidence: Failure rate: 95% of projects show no ROI
Success rate: 5% reach production with measurable profit
Experimentation rate: 80%+ use ChatGPT/Copilot
Pilot rate: 20% reach pilot stage
Production rate: 5% reach production
Average ROI for successful projects: 171% (192% in US)
Conversion rate increase: 4-7x for successful projects
Operational cost reduction: up to 80%
AI agent market size: $5.32B (2025) → projected $42.7B (2030), CAGR 41.5%
79% of organizations use AI agents
90% plan to expand usage
Vendor-built tools: 67% reach production vs. 33% for internal builds
limitations: Primary source cited is 'MIT's Project NANDA report' (link provided but not independently verified in this extraction)
Article is from no-code platform vendor (potential bias)
'95% failure' claim lacks methodology details in article
Statistics mix GenAI broadly with AI agents specifically
No breakdown by industry, company size, or use case
ZoomInfo source mentioned in search results not cited in this article

title: 6 Charts That Show The Big AI Funding Trends Of 2025
claims: Total AI investment reached $202.3 billion in 2025
AI captured close to 50% of all global funding in 2025 (up from 34% in 2024)
AI funding increased more than 75% year-over-year from $114 billion in 2024
Foundation model companies raised $80 billion (40% of global AI funding)
OpenAI valued at $500 billion (most valuable private company of all time)
Anthropic valued at $183 billion (fourth-most valuable private company)
US received 79% of AI funding ($159 billion)
San Francisco Bay Area raised $122 billion (77% of US AI funding)
evidence: Total AI funding 2025: $202.3 billion
AI funding 2024: $114 billion
Year-over-year increase: 75%+
AI share of global funding 2025: ~50%
AI share of global funding 2024: 34%
Foundation model funding 2025: $80 billion (40% of AI total)
Foundation model funding 2024: $31 billion (27% of AI total)
OpenAI valuation: $500 billion
Anthropic valuation: $183 billion
US AI funding: $159 billion (79% of total)
Bay Area AI funding: $122 billion (77% of US total)
PE-led AI deals (sole lead, $1M+): $63 billion across ~300 rounds
VC-led AI deals (sole lead, $1M+): $38 billion across ~1,600 rounds
AI megarounds ($500M+): 58% of total AI funding
limitations: Data is 'through December 2025' but exact cutoff date not specified
Methodology for categorizing 'AI sector' not detailed
Does not break down by AI application area beyond foundation models
Success/failure rates of funded companies not tracked
Geographic data limited to country/region level
